items.rationale: The house's central organizing thesis — decentralization removes information asymmetry/intermediary trust (so securities laws shouldn't apply), preserves censorship resistance, and is itself a competitive advantage. Framed as the thing that makes web3 matter and the way to survive US regulation.
items.age_note: Consistently restated 2021→2023; intensity rising with regulatory pressure

items.rationale: Seen as a new digital primitive enabling genuine ownership, creator monetization, community formation, and composable culture. Heavy market-design work on mints, auctions (clearing-price), cc0 licensing, and 'Can't Be Evil' licenses.
items.age_note: Sustained 2021-2023; framing shifted from speculation toward Pokemon-card-style durable collectibles and community design

items.rationale: Viewed as potentially as transformative as smart contracts — enabling private-by-default transactions, scaling (validity rollups), and broader DeFi/identity/gaming applications. Backed Aztec, Aleo; flagged prover-performance and security as the remaining gaps.

The scaling roadmap they endorse: rollups scale execution, danksharding/data-availability-sampling scales data, together making L2 dramatically cheaper and unlocking new applications. Diversity of optimistic and ZK rollups seen as a strength.

Held up as proof of trustless, transparent finance that performed through the 2022 collapses; investing in new primitives (Morpho peer-to-peer lending). DEXs framed as essential interoperability infrastructure for all of web3.
items.age_note: FTX/Celsius/Terra reinforced the 'DeFi over CeFi' conviction rather than denting it

items.rationale: Emerging thesis: crypto gives machines ownership and incentive structures (collective AI, crowdsourced data/models, autonomous self-funding programs). Early-stage but increasingly prominent into 2023.
items.age_note: New theme emerging late 2022, building through 2023; speculative/early

items.rationale: Token incentives to bootstrap real-world networks (telecom, compute, storage, energy) that undercut incumbents. Core test articulated as verification-cost-below-incumbent-margin; Helium cited as proof point.
User-owned game objects, interoperable assets, and fully on-chain logic seen as a new design frontier; investments in Halliday and CCP Games (EVE). Composable, player-driven economies.

Seen as fundamental new internet-native coordination/organization structures, but tempered: most DAOs over-promised, direct democracy fails, and legal wrappers (UNA) are needed. Active push for state DAO legislation.
items.age_note: Cooled from broad 2021-22 enthusiasm toward realism: governance/liability problems openly acknowledged

items.rationale: Merge-staking lets new middleware (oracles, DA, chains) borrow Ethereum's economic security instead of bootstrapping their own trust network — reframed as transformational, while flagging over-leverage/systemic risk.
items.age_note: Surfaced via 2022-08 EigenLayer talk; restated as a convergent trend Feb 2023

items.rationale: House view that the future is multi-chain; invested in LayerZero as connective infrastructure. Shared security (e.g. Superchain/Ethereum) seen as the edge over loosely-federated models.
Tokens are 'value vectors,' not equity — programmable, able to align incentives, distribute risk, and preserve decentralization. Strong distaste for tokens-as-fundraising and pie-chart 'tokenomics.'

The single dominant concern: absence of guidance since 2019, hostile enforcement under Gensler, refusal to let firms register, and deliberate fostering of uncertainty that helps bad actors and drives builders offshore. Seen as the biggest drag on the sector.
items.age_note: Escalating sharply into 2023 (Coinbase Wells, staking actions); most intense and recent concern in the corpus

items.rationale: Applying compliance obligations to autonomous protocols/DAOs is impossible and would end decentralization; the line they fight hardest to hold is regulate the app/client layer, not the protocol (the SMTP-vs-Gmail analogy).

FTX, Celsius, Voyager, BlockFi failures attributed to centralization and custody risk, not the technology. Repeated theme that 'trust must be regulated differently than trustless' and custody is a liability.
items.age_note: Crystallized after the 2022 CeFi collapses; sharpest Nov 2022

items.rationale: Treated as the clearest securities-law red line — 'non-dilutive financing' and utility-token fundraising are recurring bad ideas; predatory deal terms (short lockups, forced token launches) flagged as off-market.
items.age_note: Long-held since 2021; reaffirmed 2022-23. The 'utility token' approach declared dead after LBRY (2022-11)

items.rationale: Terra/UST collapse blamed on bad ('endogenous') collateral, not algorithms per se. Nuanced stance: well-collateralized algorithmic stablecoins are fine and important; uncollateralized ones are worthless and dangerous.
items.age_note: Position refined post-Terra: shifted blame from 'algorithmic' to 'under-collateralized/endogenous collateral'

items.rationale: Repeatedly named as the biggest direct risk to web3 users; the cyclical pattern is good-faith innovation attracting bad-faith actors and regulatory arbitrage ending in catastrophe.
Hostile/uncertain regulation pushes founders and developer activity abroad, reducing US oversight and increasing risk to US users — a recurring policy warning.

Deployed SNARKs run at 80 bits of security (FRI non-interactive) judged unsafe for systems securing hundreds of millions; prioritizing performance over security could erode trust and cause loss of funds.
Without legal wrappers, DAO members/multisig signers risk being treated as general partners or sued (e.g., tortious interference); litigation and enforcement attacks on DAOs are growing.

Splitting security across many chains/subnets makes each easier to attack crypto-economically; client concentration and over-leveraged shared stake are recurring worries even as multi-chain is embraced.
Centralized mobile gatekeepers throttle crypto UX and can block or tax native experiences — a structural distribution risk for consumer web3.

Treated as the clear category leader with unmatched developer mindshare, fee demand, and (post-Merge) economic security now securing a ~$400B economy. Also legally defended as decentralized and not a security.
items.age_note: Consistent leader call through 2022-23; Merge (Sept 2022) seen as making it strictly better

items.rationale: Respected as technically superior digital gold and the canonical decentralized, non-security asset; admiration is more analytical/structural than active investment enthusiasm. Also seen as a security/timestamping anchor for other chains.
Cited as the model for a great, decentralized token launch and as core interoperable identity/login infrastructure for web3.
items.age_note: Praised Dec 2021 and never restated as a token call; persisted, old

items.rationale: Pointed to as the trustless DeFi primitives that work — MakerDAO as the example where token governance functions; these tokens underwrite protocol risk, not merely 'governance.' a16z actively engaged in Uniswap governance.

Held up as the cautionary tale of uncollateralized / endogenously-collateralized stablecoins — 'meme coins with no upside' that collapsed; repeatedly contrasted with properly (exogenously) collateralized designs.
items.age_note: Post-May-2022 collapse; used as a recurring negative reference example through 2022

items.rationale: Widely distrusted in the legal community well before the collapse; emblematic of regulators ignoring obvious public risk under regulation-by-enforcement.
items.age_note: Concern voiced at the Nov 2022 collapse

items.rationale: The LBRY ruling treated as the death of the 'utility token' fundraising model — an ICO with no credible decentralization claim doesn't survive securities scrutiny.
Centralized issuer facing securities action; the house is skeptical of a good outcome and skeptical that the case even sets useful precedent, while noting the case law on secondaries is hard.

risk_regime.rationale: As of May 2023 the house is in a bear market but remains structurally bullish: it raised Crypto Fund 4 declaring a 'golden era of web3,' and its operative message to founders is 'stay the course' and 'keep building.' Bear markets are framed as healthy ('prolong the good faith innovation phase'). The dominant near-term emotion is not market fear but acute frustration at US regulatory hostility; capital deployment and conviction in the technology continue. Net: long-term risk-on with a defensive crouch on US regulatory exposure, CeFi counterparty risk, and bad-faith actors.

notes: Corpus is multi-author: a regulatory/legal voice (decentralization, 'regulate apps not protocols,' DAO entity structuring — the loudest and most recent signal), market-design economists (Kominers, Roughgarden, Hatfield — NFTs, auctions, vampire attacks, royalties), cryptographers (Boneh, ZK/SNARK security, danksharding), and thesis voices (Dixon, Lazzarin, Yahya — web3 networks, DePIN, crypto+AI, multi-chain). 'Consensus' here means themes that recur across these voices. Recency weighting applied: (1) the 'utility token'/ICO fundraising model is treated as reversed/dead by late 2022, not a live strategy; (2) stablecoin stance evolved from blaming 'algorithmic' to blaming bad/endogenous collateral; (3) DAO enthusiasm cooled from sweeping 2021-22 optimism to explicit governance/liability realism; (4) crypto+AI and DePIN are genuinely new (late-2022/2023) and rising. Strongest and most recent single signal in the corpus is anti-SEC / pro-decentralization regulatory advocacy. Few outright token 'picks' exist — the house is thesis- and infrastructure-driven, so ETH is the only high-conviction token call; BTC/ENS/governance-tokens are lower-intensity. Conviction scores are intensity of expressed enthusiasm/concern, not probability estimates.
